As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

My name is Darren J. Bunton, and I respectfully submit this comment for inclusion in the public record regarding the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices’ upcoming discussions on COVID-19 vaccine injuries and Long-COVID.

First, I want to acknowledge the importance of vaccination as a public health tool and the tremendous responsibility carried by the ACIP in guiding national immunization policy. Vaccines have historically prevented countless illnesses and deaths, and maintaining public trust in immunization programs is essential.

However, public trust also depends on transparency and the willingness to recognize and study adverse outcomes experienced by some individuals following vaccination.

I am among those who believe they experienced a significant health event following COVID-19 vaccination. In my case, the health changes that followed vaccination had substantial impacts on my physical well-being and daily functioning. Navigating the healthcare system to seek answers has often been difficult, as many patients reporting post-vaccination complications have encountered uncertainty, inconsistent recognition of symptoms, and limited research focused specifically on vaccine-associated injury.

Regardless of one’s position on vaccines, it is important to acknowledge that adverse events do occur in a small portion of the population. For those individuals and families affected, the consequences can be life-altering. These experiences deserve careful scientific investigation, compassionate clinical care, and policy responses that recognize both the benefits and risks associated with vaccination.

As ACIP evaluates COVID-19 vaccine injuries and Long-COVID, I respectfully urge the committee to consider the following:

1. Strengthening research into vaccine-associated adverse events, including mechanisms that may explain why some individuals experience prolonged or severe reactions.

2. Improving clinical recognition and treatment pathways for individuals experiencing persistent post-vaccination symptoms.

3. Enhancing transparency in safety monitoring systems, including clearer communication about how adverse event data are collected, evaluated, and acted upon.

4. Ensuring that individuals who experience serious adverse outcomes have access to meaningful medical support and compensation pathways.

A balanced and transparent approach to vaccine safety ultimately strengthens public confidence. Acknowledging and studying vaccine injuries does not undermine vaccination programs; rather, it reinforces scientific integrity and public trust.

I appreciate the opportunity to submit this comment and hope that the committee will continue to evaluate these issues with rigor, compassion, and openness to the experiences of patients.

Respectfully submitted,
Darren J. Bunton
Indianapolis, Indiana

As an infectious diseases medical professional, I urge ACIP to once again serve as a source of credible, evidence-based information to inform vaccine coverage. Current members lacking vaccine expertise and appointed without transparency should be dismissed, and new members with the necessary qualifications should be appointed per the ACIP charter and federal law. Until ACIP is trustworthy and scientifically sound, it should not meet.

ACIP must base recommendations on high-quality, objective, scientific data analyzed by CDC scientists and experts in infectious diseases and vaccinology. Recommendations made by ACIP impact my patients, their communities and health care nationwide, and should be grounded in evidence, not politics. 

In response to the IOM, in 2010 ACIP formally adopted the Grading of Recommendations Assessment, Development and Evaluation (GRADE) framework for systematic evidence review and evaluation of benefits and harms. ACIP adopted the Evidence to Recommendation (EtR) framework in 2018, adding structured evaluation of public health, benefits and harms, costs, feasibility, stakeholder perspectives and equity.

ACIP should adhere to the systematic, objective and transparent process of GRADE and the EtR framework for decision making during work group meetings and at its meetings. Any changes to the ACIP recommendation methodology should be released publicly and developed using input from ACIP liaisons, CDC subject matter experts and other stakeholders. ACIP should reject any methodology change that would permit recommendations to proceed without a documented GRADE evidence assessment and a completed EtR framework. Any changes to methodology should not compromise decision making based on scientific evidence and research conducted by experts in the field.

COVID-19 vaccine injuries and long COVID:
I encourage ACIP members to ensure they are remaining true to the purview of the Committee, and the specific responsibilities they have to vaccines and immunizations, as stated in the ACIP charter "[to] provide advice for the control of diseases for which a vaccine is licensed in the U.S." The proposed topic of long COVID falls outside of this purview, unless the Committee plans to discuss the clear and consistent evidence that the COVID-19 vaccine reduces the risk of long COVID. While the Committee should consider vaccine risks and demonstrated side effects while deliberating on recommendations, vaccine injuries themselves do not directly fall within the mandates of ACIP. ACIP should fulfill its mandate in focusing on evidence-based vaccine review and recommendations and utilize existing safety systems to identify validated safety signals.

The Countermeasures Injury Compensation Program administered by the Health Resources and Services Administration is the best avenue for the small percentage of patients with severe adverse reactions to the COVID-19 vaccination. While these injuries should be considered as an aspect of recommendations, it must be in the context of the widespread benefit COVID-19 vaccines have provided in the United States and avoidance of severe disease and hospitalizations from COVID-19. Rigorous data have demonstrated the safety and effectiveness of COVID-19 vaccines across age groups and populations. Casting doubt on their safety will only cause more confusion for patients and could depress vaccine uptake, leaving more people at risk of long COVID, hospitalization and death.

I object to changes being made to COVID vaccine recommendations or any other vaccine recommendations without proper consideration and evaluation by vaccine experts.

Thank you for your attention.

To the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices,

I am writing to urge the committee to make COVID-19 vaccine recommendations that are specific to vendors and platforms, and in particular to support expanding the Nuvaxovid indication to include pediatric use.

mRNA vaccines should not be the only option available to children. Families deserve the ability to choose from multiple vaccine platforms, and protein-based options like Novavax offer a meaningful alternative. Children need robust protection from both post-vaccination syndrome and Long COVID, and broadening access to different vaccine technologies is essential to achieving that.

Novavax's protein-based platform offers several distinct clinical advantages: a broadened antigenic range, greater durability of immune response, mucosal immunity, a favorable IgG1/IgG3 antibody profile, fewer reported side effects, and longer-lasting protection compared to mRNA vaccines. These benefits are particularly relevant for pediatric populations, where parental confidence in safety and tolerability directly affects uptake.

I also encourage the committee to support next-generation saponin-based adjuvants such as Novavax's Matrix-M. These adjuvants have demonstrated strong safety profiles and enable vaccines that require less antigen per dose — an important consideration for pediatric formulations.

Finally, I ask the committee to preserve a shared decision-making framework that includes both Novavax and mRNA vaccines, and to ensure that all COVID-19 vaccines remain available to every American with no out-of-pocket costs. Cost barriers disproportionately affect children in underserved communities, and equitable access must remain a priority.

Thank you for your consideration.

Dear Members of the ACIP,

I am writing to urge the committee to support expanding the Nuvaxovid indication to include pediatric use. Children currently have no alternative to mRNA COVID-19 vaccines. Parents deserve the freedom to choose between vaccine platforms for their children, and the evidence supports giving them that choice.

Children remain at risk for Long COVID, and families should not be locked into a single-platform approach when a safe, proven alternative exists. The committee should preserve shared decision-making between parents and providers and ensure that individual families — not a one-size-fits-all recommendation — determine what is best for their children.

Please recommend Nuvaxovid for pediatric use.

Thank you.

My son, who was 40 years old at the time, shared an office with another employee who had taken the "vaccine."  He did not take the shot.  He developed severe Bell's Palsy which was treated with Prednisone, and it resolved in a few weeks.  A few weeks subsequent to that, he was swimming in a lake for about and hour when he developed unmistakable Pulmonary Edema  (PE).  I am a retired RN. My family and my husband were in Idaho to celebrate our 50th wedding anniversary.  My son had gone swimming right after we arrived at the venue. About an hour later, I was called to the dock which was about 60 feet down from the house.  When I evaluated him, I could clearly see that he had the PE but I was baffled at what may have caused it.  He had no peripheral edema, and, though I had read about "foaming at the mouth" with PE, I had never in my long career witnessed it.  I had oxygen in my car and had it brought to the dock.  I called 911 and requested and emergency transport by boat as we were so far downhill from the road and my son was a big man.  We did finally get a fire boat rescue with 5 or 6 emergency medical staff to help transfer him to the hospital.  In the ER, he was diagnosed with Swimming-Induced Pulmonary Edema (SIPE), a condition of which I had never heard.  The ER doctor stated that she had seen 3 cases of this in the past week.  My son was hospitalized for three days (our entire vacation).  He had no insurance so continues to owe on that hospital stay.  He took it easy as he recovered from the incident.  The hospital apparently discharged him with a diagnosis of pneumonia which he clearly did not have. He was in good health, afebrile, and completely normal before swimming.  His case is important as it is illustrative of shedding injuries.  The fact that the ER physician had seen three cases of this quite unusual condition in one week is suggestive of an unusual cause which I have concluded is likely related to the "vaccine" from direct administration or shedding.  I have no supportive data or files as my son is an adult.  I am sure that if you would like supportive data/files I can assist in procurement of them.
